coredata.dc:title: A dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor based on N, P-CDs and shape transformation of AgNPrs for the determination of 6-mercaptopurine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Salari, Rana
Hallaj, Tooba
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we designed a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor by using nitrogen and phosphor doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) and Ag nanoprisms (AgNPrs) to detect 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). For this purpose, we applied the AgNPrs/I- mixture to establish a shape transformation based colorimetric method for the detection of 6-MP. The assay mechanism of colorimetric method was based on etching and protecting effect of I- and 6-MP on the AgNPrs. In the presence of I-, as an etching agent, the solution color altered from blue to purple and the position of AgNPrs' local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak shifted to the blue wavelengths. This phenomenon was assigned to the morphological change of AgNPrs. In the presence of 6-MP, AgNPrs were protected from etching by I-, so the LSPR peak position and solution color of AgNPrs remained unchangeable. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs decreased with adding AgNPrs/I- due to the spectral overlap between etched AgNPrs and N, P-CDs. The CDs' quenched fluorescence was restored in the presence of 6-MP, as a result of the protecting effect of 6-MP on the AgNPrs. These facts have been applied to develop a dual sensor for the determination of 6-MP at the range of 10–500 nM and 30–500 nM by colorimetric and fluorometric detection methods. The detection limits were obtained 10 and 4 nM for fluorometric and colorimetric methods, respectively. The developed sensor was utilized for dual signal analysis of 6-MP in human serum samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode sensor
Anisotropic nanoparticles
Doped carbon dots
Inner filter effect
6-mercaptopurine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of AgNPrs and N, P-CDs
Analysis of 6-MP in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Urmia University of Medical Sciences for the support provided (The project number: 2723 ). This project approved by the Research Ethic Committee at the Urmia University of Medical Sciences , Ethical code: IR. UMSU.REC.1399.064 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, P-CDs, (b) FT-IR spectra, and (c) EDS spectra of N, P-CDs.

(A) TEM image of AgNPrs, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AgNPrs (a) alone, (b) in the presence of I-, (c) in the presence of I- and 6-MP, and (d) in the presence of 6-MP. conditions: AgNPrs (500 μL), pH = 10, (glycine buffer, 2.5 mM), I- (5 μM), 6-MP (0.5 μM), time (5 min).
TEM image of (a) AgNPrs/I- and (b) AgNPrs/6-MP/ I-.

Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs (a) alone, (b) in the presence of AgNPrs, (c) in the presence of AgNPrs and I-, (d) in the presence of AgNPrs, I- and 6-MP. Conditions: pH = 10, (glycine buffer, 2.5 mM), I- (5 μM), incubation time (5 min), and AgNPrs (500 μL), 6-MP (0.5 μM), N, P-CDs (200 μL).

(a) Absorption spectra of AgNPrs in the presence of I- and different concentrations of 6-MP, (b) The recovered fluorescence of N, P-CDs/AgNPrs/I- system in the presence of various concentrations of 6-MP. (ΔI = I-I0, is the difference between the fluorescence intensity in the presence of 6-MP (I) and in its abscnces (I0)), Conditions: pH = 10, (glycine buffer, 2.5 mM), I- (5 μM), incubation time (5 min), and AgNPrs (500 μL), 6-MP (0.5 μM), N, P-CDs (200 μL).

Effect of some potentially interfering species in determination of 100 nM 6-MP by proposed (a) colorimetric and (b) fluorometric method. Except ascorbic acid and 6-thioguanine which were 500 nM, the concentration of other species was 2000 nM.
Comparison between the proposed method for determination of 6-MP with some published methods.
Determination of 6-MP in serum samples by colorimetric probe.
Determination of 6-MP in serum by fluorometric probe.

head.ce:title: A dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor based on N, P-CDs and shape transformation of AgNPrs for the determination of 6-mercaptopurine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we designed a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor by using nitrogen and phosphor doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) and Ag nanoprisms (AgNPrs) to detect 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). For this purpose, we applied the AgNPrs/I- mixture to establish a shape transformation based colorimetric method for the detection of 6-MP. The assay mechanism of colorimetric method was based on etching and protecting effect of I- and 6-MP on the AgNPrs. In the presence of I-, as an etching agent, the solution color altered from blue to purple and the position of AgNPrs' local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak shifted to the blue wavelengths. This phenomenon was assigned to the morphological change of AgNPrs. In the presence of 6-MP, AgNPrs were protected from etching by I-, so the LSPR peak position and solution color of AgNPrs remained unchangeable. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs decreased with adding AgNPrs/I- due to the spectral overlap between etched AgNPrs and N, P-CDs. The CDs' quenched fluorescence was restored in the presence of 6-MP, as a result of the protecting effect of 6-MP on the AgNPrs. These facts have been applied to develop a dual sensor for the determination of 6-MP at the range of 10–500 nM and 30–500 nM by colorimetric and fluorometric detection methods. The detection limits were obtained 10 and 4 nM for fluorometric and colorimetric methods, respectively. The developed sensor was utilized for dual signal analysis of 6-MP in human serum samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP), known as the first of thiopurine analogs, has been clinically applied in cancer therapy especially for the treatment of childhood acute leukemia. 6-MP can inhibit the proliferation of the cancer cell via inhibition of de novo purine nucleotide synthesis and incorporation of triphosphate into RNA and DNA [1]. However, same as other chemotherapy drugs, it can cause some serious side effects because of its toxicity, resulting in myelosuppression, immunosuppression, and hepatotoxicity [2]. Therefore, control and monitoring amount of 6-MP in the human body is of considerable importance and several analytical techniques including electrochemical and chromatographic methods [3–5] as well as colorimetric and fluorometric sensors [6–10] have been reported for analysis of this anti-cancer agent in biological fluids. Some of these methods are time-consuming, complicate and expensive, so establishing a simple, sensitive and cast-effective technique for the determination of 6-MP would be interesting in biological analysis. Currently, colorimetric and fluorometric dual sensors have been attracted significant attention in the pharmaceutical and biological assays because of their reliability and sensitivity [11–14]. In these sensors, since two different methods are exploited to measure the same analytes the obtained results would be so reliable. On the other hand, these dual-mode sensors can take advantages of both methods simultaneously, such as high sensitivity of fluorescence assays and the naked-eye detection of colorimetry method. A variety of fluorophores such as organic dyes [15], Si-NPs [13], and graphene QDs [14], and carbon QDs [11,16] with different noble metal nanostructures have been exploited to establish dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensors. Concerning the desirable photoluminescence properties of N, P co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) and their spectral overlap with AuNPs, we found these CDs as a suitable choice for the development of a dual sensor for the determination of uric acid in our previous work [17]. N, P-CDs have indicated altered and promoted photophysical properties due to the creation of substitutional defects in their structure due to doping CDs by N and P, as an n-type donor [18,19]. N, P-CDs have been applied in various fields including the development of fluorometric sensors for the determination of several compounds such as curcumin, Cd(II) and Fe(III) [20–22].

On the other hand, anisotropic metal nanoparticles including Ag nanoprisms (AgNPrs) have been widely exploited to design shape transformation based colorimetric methods due to their exceptional local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) properties [23–26]. The main principle for establishment of these methods is the dependence of LSPR peak position and solution color to the component, size, shape and surrounding environment of metal nanoparticles [27]. It is worth mentioning that due to desirable selectivity and lower matrices effect, shape transformation based colorimetric methods have been found more suitable than aggregation based colorimetric methods for biological analysis [28]. Although, AgNPrs have been widely exploited to establish various colorimetric methods, there are rare studies on the fluorometric and dual sensors based on AgNPrs [29–31].
Herein, AgNPrs and N, P-CDs were exploited to establish a shape transformation based colorimetric and turn-off–on fluorometric sensor for analysis of 6-MP in human biological fluids.

The shape and size of AgNPrs and N,P-CDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips EM 208, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; Tensor 27, Bruker) spectrophotometer was applied for recording FT-IR spectrum. The UV/Vis and fluorescence analyses were performed by Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) and FP‐8300 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan), respectively.

Silver nitrate, citric acid, phosphoric acid, trisodium citrate, glycine, formamide, hydrogen peroxide, sodium borohydride and potassium iodide were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP, MW ~ 10,000 g mol−1) and 6-MP were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

To prepare AgNPrs, 240 μL hydrogen peroxide (30w/w %) was injected to 100 mL aqua solution containing AgNO3 (0.1 mM), trisodium citrate dehydrate (1.8 mM), and PVP (0.042 mM) which was prepared in ultra-pure water. After 10 min vigorously stirring at room temperature, 1.0 mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 (100 mM) was added to the obtained solution. With adding NaBH4, a pale-yellow color appeared in the solution which gently altered from pale yellow to red, green and blue, during around 40 min stirring at room temperature. The appearance of blue color in the solution indicated the formation of AgNPrs which aged overnight and stored at 4 °C for following works [24].

N, P-CDs were hydrothermally prepared according to the previously reported method [17]. For this purpose, 0.18 g urea and 0.21 g citric acid were solved in the mixture of 2.5 mL formamide and 2.5 mL phosphoric acid. The obtained solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated for 4 h at 160 °C. Afterward, it was naturally cooled down to room temperature. To remove the large size particles and purify the obtained solution, it was centrifuged and dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h. The prepared N, P-CDs were diluted to 25 mL and kept at 4 °C for further experiments.

For colorimetric measurements, 500 μL of AgNPrs, 25 μL glycine buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 10) and a suitable volume of 6-MP standard or sample solution were incubated into a test tube for 5 min. Then, 10 μL I- 0.5 mM was added to the solution and the final volume was reached to 1.0 mL with deionized water. After keeping at room temperature for 5 min, the absorption spectrum of obtained solution was recorded in a micro-volume cuvette. For fluorometric assays, 200 µL N, P-CDs was added to the final obtained solution of colorimetric part, and fluorescence measurement was done at 400 nm excitation wavelength.

To precipitate serum proteins 2 mL of barium hydroxide and 1.8 mL of zinc sulfate 0.1 M were added to a test tube containing 500 μL spiked human serum with an appropriate amount of 6-MP sample. After centrifuging the obtained mixture at 4000 ramps for 20 min, the clear supernatant was transferred to 5 mL volumetric flask and reached to the final volume with deionized water. At last, a suitable amount of the solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AgNPrs and N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Green emitting N, P-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method from urea, phosphoric acid and citric acid as N, P and C precursors. TEM image of N, P-CDs indicates that they are spherical nanoparticles with a size distribution 6 ± 3 nm (Fig. 1 a, S1a). FT-IR and EDS analysis were applied for the investigation of the functional groups and present elements in the structure of N, P-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum confirmed the existence of various functional groups including C = C, C = O, C = N, C-N, NH, P = O, P-O-C, P-OH, NH2 and OH in the structure of N, P-CDs. The related peaks were indicated in (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, according to the EDS results (Fig. 1c) N, P-CDs contain 47, 15 and 37% of N, P and C elements, respectively. The optical properties of N, P-CDs were examined by recording their UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S1, Electronic supplementary information (ESI)). The obtained N,P-CDs indicated excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior at the range of λex = 380–420 nm, with the maximum emission wavelength around 525 nm (Fig. S1b, ESI).

On the other hand, TEM imaging and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy were utilized to characterize as-prepared AgNPrs. TEM image (Fig. 2 a) reveals that AgNPrs are in triangular shape and size distribution of (38 ± 7 nm). Additionally, three SPR characteristic peaks of AgNPrs appeared at the absorption spectrum of AgNPrs, Fig. 2b. The peaks positioned at 330, 490 and 752 nm can be assigned to out-of-plane and in-plane quadrupole as well as in-plane dipole plasmon resonance of AgNPrs, respectively.

A dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor was designed by obtained N, P-CDs and AgNPrs to detect 6-MP. In this sensor, the effect of I- , as an etching agent, was investigated on the AgNPrs, as colorimetric reporters. As shown in Fig. 2b, the solution color and the LSPR peak position of AgNPrs were changed by adding I- as a result of shape transformation of AgNPrs. It is worth mentioning that the surface energy and activity of Ag atoms at the corner site of AgNPrs is high, assigned to the low coordination number of Ag atoms [32]. Therefore, corner site placed Ag atoms can be easily dissociated by I-, leading to the morphological transformation of AgNPrs. We observed that in the presence of 6-MP the LSPR peak position of AgNPrs red-shifted and the solution color returned to the blueish (Fig. 2b). It can be ascribed to the protecting effect of 6-MP on the AgNPrs due to its sulfur containing structure. The reported Ksp for Ag2S (1.6 × 10−49) and AgI (8.49 × 10−17) indicates that the Ag-S band is so stronger than Ag-I band [25], accordingly, I- ions are not be able to attack the surface of AgNPrs in the presence of 6-MP. Indeed, the affinity of Ag atoms to sulfur is higher than I-, resulting in the formation of Ag-S bond at the corner sites and protection of AgNPrs from etching by I- [33].

To achieve the highest sensitivity of designed sensor, we optimized some effective factors in the measurements, including pH, the concentration of I-, incubation time and amount of AgNPrs. The obtained results was given in Fig. S3 and S4. We found that under optimized condition (pH = 10, (glycine buffer, 2.5 mM), I- (5 μM), incubation time (5 min), and AgNPrs (500 μL)), there is a linear correlation between wavelength variations (Δλ) of AgNPrs' LSPR peak and the logarithm of 6-MP concentration at the range of 10–500 nM. The inset picture also indicates that with rising the 6-MP concentration at the mentioned range, the color change of solution from purple to blue can be observed even with naked eyes. Otherwise, the recovered fluorescence signal of N, P-CDs/AgNPrs/I- system (ΔI) was proportional with the concentration logarithm of 6-MP from 30 to 500 nM Fig. 5 b. The detection limits (3σ) of colorimetric and fluorometric methods were obtained to be 4 and 10 nM, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.6% and 3.5% for five replicate determinations of 100 nM 6-MP for colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

Furthermore, the selectivity of developed sensor was investigated for the determination of 100 nM 6-MP in the presence of various ions and compounds which can be found in biological fluids. The results reported in Fig. 6 confirmed the acceptable selectivity of the dual sensor for detection of 6-MP in biological fluids. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , 500 nM 6-thioguanin (6-TG: the other thiopurine applied as an anticancer drug) indicates negligible interference on the measurement of 6-MP. It can be attributed to the presence of an amine group in the 6-TG structure which results in more intermolecular hydrogen bonds and less accessibility to sulfurs atom compared to the 6-MP [34].
Additionally, compared to the previously reported methods, including colorimetric or/ and fluorometric methods, our method indicated desirable analytical performance (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of 6-MP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the applicability of developed sensor in real sample analysis, both colorimetric and fluorometric methods were utilized for the determination of 6-MP in human serum samples. The obtained results were summarized in Table 2 and 3
. The recoveries and t-student test results confirmed the acceptable performance of established sensor for real sample analysis.

To sum up, a dual signal colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for 6-MP monitoring was established based on N, P-CDs, AgNPrs and I- . The colorimetric detection part was based on the shape transformation of AgNPrs as a result of etching/ anti-etching effect of I- and 6-MP, as etching and protecting agents, respectively. We indicated that 6-MP can protect AgNPrs from etching by I- , resulting in the prevention of LSPR peak blue shift of AgNPrs. The variation of LSPR peak position was proportional to the concentration of 6-MP. On the other hand, due to the significant overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of N, P-CDs and the absorption spectrum of AgNPrs/I- , the fluorescence of N, P-CDs was quenched with adding AgNPrs/I- as a result of IFE. However, in the presence of 6-MP/AgNPrs/I-, the quenched fluorescence of N, P-CDs was recovered as a result of the spectral overlap decrease between N, P-CDs and AgNPrs (protected by 6-MP). As well as the variation of LSPR peak position, the fluorescence recovery was proportional to the amount of 6-MP. Based on these facts, a sensitive colorimetric and fluorometric sensor was developed for the analysis of 6-MP in biological fluid with satisfactory results.
Rana Salari: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Tooba Hallaj: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Urmia University of Medical Sciences for the support provided (The project number: 2723). This project approved by the Research Ethic Committee at the Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Ethical code: IR. UMSU.REC.1399.064).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
